lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 63: Redundant Indirect Object `le/les`
lessons.targetPattern: Use redundant indirect object pronouns le and les with high-frequency indirect-object verbs even when the recipient is explicitly named with a plus person.
lessons.englishTrap: Removing le or les when recipient is already mentioned.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish commonly keeps indirect object pronouns with many verbs regardless of whether a plus recipient phrase is present.
formula: core frame: le or les plus verb plus direct object plus a plus person
recipient can be omitted while pronoun remains
le marks singular recipient, les marks plural recipients
high-frequency verbs: dar decir contar comprar traer mandar enviar pedir robar quitar prestar devolver
pronoun appears before conjugated verb in standard order
naturalExamples: Le di el libro a Juan.
Le dije la verdad a Maria.
Le mande una carta a Pedro.
Les prestamos el coche a mis vecinos.
Le quitaron el telefono a Marta.

controlled.instructions: Write each in natural Spanish using redundant le or les.
prompts: I gave the keys to Carlos.
I told Maria the truth.
We told a story to our son.
I bought a gift for my wife.
She brought flowers to her mother.
I sent a message to Pedro.
They asked Luisa for a favor.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 62: Que Yo Sepa / Que Yo Recuerde
lessons.targetPattern: Use fixed sentence starters que yo sepa and que yo recuerde to express limited certainty naturally while using stable subjunctive chunk forms.
lessons.englishTrap: Responding with rigid certainty or pausing awkwardly when unsure.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish uses fixed hedging chunks to sound natural and fluent under uncertainty. These expressions are memorized as complete units.
formula: knowledge hedge: que yo sepa plus statement
memory hedge: que yo recuerde plus statement
both expressions contain fixed subjunctive forms
typical use is sentence-initial with pause
best practice is chunk memorization not word-by-word assembly
naturalExamples: Que yo sepa, vive en Guadalajara.
Que yo recuerde, trabaja en el supermercado.
Que yo sepa, la reunion es a las diez.
Que yo recuerde, no hubo cambios.
Que yo sepa, el proyecto sigue activo.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 61: Ir vs Irse vs Salir
lessons.targetPattern: Distinguish ir for destination, irse for departure, and salir for exiting, going out, release events, and outcome expressions like me salio bien.
lessons.englishTrap: Using one leave/go translation for all movement meanings.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish separates movement verbs by focus: destination ir, departure irse, and exit or outcome salir.
formula: destination: ir plus a plus place
departure: irse with reflexive pronoun
origin or exit: salir de plus place
social and activity: salir a plus infinitive and salir con plus person
outcome: indirect object pronoun plus salir plus adjective or adverb
naturalExamples: Voy a la oficina.
Ya me voy.
Salgo de casa a las ocho.
Quieres salir a cenar?
La entrevista me salio bien.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 60: Preposition-Front Questions
lessons.targetPattern: In Spanish questions, required verb prepositions move to the front before que, and interrogative-word questions place verb before an expressed subject.
lessons.englishTrap: Copying English question order and leaving prepositions at the end.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish front-loads required prepositions in questions and typically uses interrogative plus verb plus subject order when a question word is present.
formula: preposition-front question: de or en plus que plus verb
interrogative order with expressed subject: que plus verb plus subject
yes-no questions allow both verb-subject and subject-verb orders
learn verb plus preposition as one unit: pensar en, hablar de, quejarse de, arrepentirse de, disfrazarse de
naturalExamples: En que piensas?
De que se queja Maria?
De que quieres hablar?
Que dijo ella?
Vive Maria aqui?

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 59: Verb + Preposition Chunks
lessons.targetPattern: Use high-frequency Spanish verb plus preposition chunks as fixed patterns, including meaning shifts with pensar and pronominal constructions like quejarse de and enamorarse de.
lessons.englishTrap: Choosing prepositions by literal English translation.
lessons.spanishLogic: Many Spanish verbs require specific prepositions as part of one chunk. Changing the preposition can change meaning or sound non-native.
formula: sonar con plus noun or infinitive
pensar plus infinitive for plans, pensar en for mental focus, pensar de for opinion
pronominal chunks: enamorarse de, despedirse de, cansarse de, burlarse de, quejarse de
action chunks: invitar a, aprender a, ensenar a, empezar a, ayudar a, limitarse a
support and dependence chunks: contar con, depender de, acostumbrarse a, abusar de
naturalExamples: Sueno con vivir cerca del mar.
Pienso mudarme el proximo ano.
Siempre pienso en mis padres.
Que piensas del nuevo supervisor?
Puedes contar conmigo.

He abuses his body with little sleep.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 58: Five Core Uses of `Lo`
lessons.targetPattern: Master five high-frequency lo constructions: direct object lo, neuter lo plus adjective, lo que relative clauses, emphatic lo plus adjective or adverb plus que plus subjunctive, and fixed idiomatic expressions with lo.
lessons.englishTrap: Expecting lo to map to one English word in all contexts.
lessons.spanishLogic: Lo changes function by structure. Read the whole chunk to interpret meaning accurately and respond naturally.
formula: direct object: lo replaces masculine singular object noun
abstract neuter article: lo plus adjective
relative abstract clause: lo que
emphatic degree: lo plus adjective or adverb plus que plus subjunctive
idiomatic chunks: a lo largo de, a lo lejos, lo antes posible, a lo que parece
naturalExamples: Lo conoci ayer.
Lo dificil es empezar.
Lo que necesito es descanso.
No importa lo cansado que estes.
Necesito la respuesta lo antes posible.

controlled.instructions: Write each in natural Spanish using the correct lo pattern.

Send me the file as soon as possible.
Apparently, they canceled the meeting.
I did not understand what he said.
The curious thing is that nobody noticed it.
He bought the book, but he has not read it yet.
What I like most is listening to podcasts.
It does not matter how intelligent they are.
answers: Lo conoci ayer.
Ya lo lei.
Lo bueno es que terminamos a tiempo.
Lo dificil es decidir.
Lo que quiero es un lugar tranquilo.
Ella siempre hace lo que promete.
Nunca sabes lo que tienes hasta que lo pierdes.

No importa lo ocupado que estes.
No importa lo rapido que manejes.
No importa lo cansado que estes, sigue adelante.
Caminamos a lo largo de la playa.
Vi una luz a lo lejos.
Mandame el archivo lo antes posible.
A lo que parece, cancelaron la reunion.
No entendi lo que dijo.
Lo curioso es que nadie lo noto.
Compro el libro, pero todavia no lo ha leido.
Lo que mas me gusta es escuchar podcasts.
No importa lo inteligentes que sean.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 57: Using `lo` to Refer Back
lessons.targetPattern: Use neutral lo to refer back to previously mentioned qualities manner or actions, while recognizing short factual identity replies where lo is often omitted.
lessons.englishTrap: Repeating adjectives and full ideas instead of using compact reference structures.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish uses neutral lo to refer back to qualities adverbial manner and whole actions. This lo is invariable and does not match gender or number.
formula: quality reply: si lo es or no no lo es
plural subject with neutral reference: si lo son
manner or action echo: si lo hago or si lo hace
whole previous action echo: si lo hizo
short factual identity often omits lo: si es or no no es
naturalExamples: Maria es muy inteligente. Si, lo es.
Juan y Carlos son guapos. Si, lo son.
Es aburrida la pelicula? No, no lo es.
Miguel hizo un excelente trabajo. Si, lo hizo.
Juan es profesor? Si, es.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 56: Prepositions + Infinitive for Faster Speech
lessons.targetPattern: Use prepositions plus infinitive to connect actions fluently, and switch to para que plus subjunctive when purpose clauses involve a different subject.
lessons.englishTrap: Conjugating every second verb and losing fluency in real time.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish uses preposition plus infinitive chunks to link actions quickly. For purpose with same subject use para plus infinitive, but with subject change use para que plus subjunctive.
formula: al plus infinitive for upon doing
antes de or despues de plus infinitive for sequencing
en vez de or en lugar de plus infinitive for substitution
sin plus infinitive for without doing
para plus infinitive same subject, para que plus subjunctive different subject
naturalExamples: Al llegar a casa, me acoste.
Antes de salir, compre pan.
Despues de comer, volvi al trabajo.
Se fue sin despedirse.
Apague la luz para que los ninos durmieran.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 55: Tan, Tanto, Mucho, Demasiado
lessons.targetPattern: Use tan with adjectives and adverbs, use tanto mucho and demasiado as adjective or adverb depending on context, and apply tan como and tanto como for quality and quantity comparisons.
lessons.englishTrap: Using one generic so/much form without changing structure by function.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish separates degree and quantity. Tan modifies adjectives or adverbs, while tanto mucho demasiado can modify nouns with agreement or verbs as invariable adverbs.
formula: degree: tan plus adjective or adverb
noun quantity: tanto/tanta/tantos/tantas plus noun
adverbial amount: verb plus tanto or mucho or demasiado
quality comparison: tan plus adjective or adverb plus como
quantity or action comparison: tanto plus noun plus como or verb plus tanto como
naturalExamples: Este libro es tan util.
Te quiero tanto.
Hay muchos problemas aqui.
Ella habla demasiado en las reuniones.
Juan trabaja tanto como Maria.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 54: Cada Vez Patterns
lessons.targetPattern: Use cada vez que for repeated events and cada vez mas or menos or mejor or peor for gradual change, including combined structures like cada vez que X, Y cada vez mas.
lessons.englishTrap: Using separate literal phrases for every time and gradual change instead of one reusable Spanish frame.
lessons.spanishLogic: Cada vez acts as a flexible frame for repetition and progression. Add que for event clauses, and combine with mas menos mejor peor for change over time.
formula: repetition event: cada vez que plus verb plus main clause
increase: cada vez mas plus noun adjective or adverb
decrease: cada vez menos plus noun adjective or adverb
quality progression: cada vez mejor or cada vez peor
combined pattern: cada vez que X, Y cada vez mas or menos
naturalExamples: Cada vez que te veo, me rio.
La ciudad esta cada vez mas cara.
Tengo cada vez menos tiempo libre.
Mi espanol esta cada vez mejor.
Cada vez que practico, entiendo cada vez mas.

Base: Vemos a nuestros amigos cada vez menos. -> change adverb to mas seguido.
Base: Cada vez hay mas gente aqui. -> change to decrease.
Base: Cada vez que cocino, sale mejor. -> change verb to trabajar.
Base: Duermo cada vez menos entre semana. -> change to first person plural.
Base: Cada vez que hablamos, discutimos. -> make it positive result with nos entendemos.
Base: El trafico esta cada vez mas pesado. -> change noun to trabajo and adjective to intenso.
Base: Cada vez que practico, cometo menos errores. -> change object to dudas.
Base: Mi concentracion esta cada vez peor. -> change noun to mi memoria and direction to improvement.
Base: Cada vez salimos mas a menudo. -> change to decrease frequency.
answers: Cada vez que te veo, me pongo nervioso.
La ciudad esta cada vez mas cara.
Tengo cada vez menos energia.
Mi espanol esta cada vez peor.
Cada vez que estudio, entiendo cada vez mas.

La ciudad esta cada vez mas cara.

Nos vemos cada vez menos.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 53: Future Tense for Present Speculation
lessons.targetPattern: Use future forms like sera estara llamara and querra to express present speculation uncertainty and rhetorical wondering rather than literal future time.
lessons.englishTrap: Treating future tense only as later time and missing speculative meaning in present context.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish future forms can signal uncertainty right now in questions and reactions. This gives a natural might/could/I wonder tone.
formula: identity guess: quien sera
location guess: donde estara
action guess: quien llamara or quien estara tocando
interpretation guess: que querra decir con eso
naturalExamples: Quien sera a esta hora?
Quien llamara tan tarde?
Donde estara Juan?
Que querra decir con eso?
Por que estara tan callada hoy?

Quien llamara?
Donde estara Marta?

Por que estaran tarde?
Quien estara en la puerta?
Donde estaran mis llaves?

commonErrors.mistake: Future form interpreted only as literal future time
commonErrors.why_it_happens: Learners memorize one default meaning for future tense.
commonErrors.correct_spanish: In context sera and estara can express present speculation.

commonErrors.mistake: Present indicative used in rhetorical guessing questions
commonErrors.why_it_happens: Word-for-word transfer from English questions.
commonErrors.correct_spanish: Use speculative future when you are genuinely unsure.

commonErrors.mistake: Overusing speculative future in normal factual questions
commonErrors.why_it_happens: New pattern is overgeneralized.
commonErrors.correct_spanish: Reserve this pattern for uncertainty or wondering tone.

commonErrors.mistake: Avoiding querra decir because it feels advanced
commonErrors.why_it_happens: Preference for literal quiere decir only.
commonErrors.correct_spanish: Que querra decir con eso is common and natural.

commonErrors.why_it_happens: Low familiarity with forms during fast writing.
commonErrors.correct_spanish: Keep forms consistent in your output format for practice.

commonErrors.mistake: No distinction between certainty and speculation

commonErrors.correct_spanish: Future speculation softens certainty and adds doubt.

commonErrors.mistake: Trying to force subjunctive for every uncertainty meaning

commonErrors.mistake: Building only statements and avoiding question form

commonErrors.correct_spanish: Use present or past when facts are already known.

commonErrors.why_it_happens: Future morphology is auto-interpreted as later time.
commonErrors.correct_spanish: Train recognition that many sera/estara lines refer to now.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 52: Deber (Direct Obligation vs Soft Advice)
lessons.targetPattern: Use deber in present for direct obligation and deberia/deberias in conditional for softer suggestions or recommendations, with deber plus infinitive as the core structure.
lessons.englishTrap: Using one generic should pattern and missing tone differences.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish uses tense choice in deber to adjust force: present for stronger obligation and conditional for softer advice.
formula: direct obligation: deber in present plus infinitive
soft recommendation: deberia/deberias/deberiamos/deberian plus infinitive
self-talk advice frame: yo deberia plus infinitive
add subject when needed because deberia can be ambiguous
naturalExamples: Debes llamar ahora.
Deberias llamar hoy.
Yo deberia ordenar mi oficina.
Deberiamos comer mas sano.
Ustedes deberian salir mas temprano.

Suggestion to yourself. (Debo acostarme mas temprano. vs Deberia acostarme mas temprano.)
Softer proposal in a meeting. (Debemos cambiar la estrategia. vs Deberiamos cambiar la estrategia.)

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 51: Time Clauses That Trigger Subjunctive
lessons.targetPattern: Use subjunctive after cuando, en cuanto, and una vez que for future-dependent or command contexts; use indicative for habitual routines; use subjunctive after antes de que; and use imperfect subjunctive in past-time dependent narration.
lessons.englishTrap: Using present indicative after when/as soon as/once in all contexts.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish separates routine facts from pending dependent actions by mood. Pending future or command-linked actions trigger subjunctive, while habitual repeated actions use indicative.
formula: future or command dependence: main clause plus cuando/en cuanto/una vez que plus subjunctive
habitual routine: main clause plus cuando/en cuanto/una vez que plus indicative
antes de que always takes subjunctive
past narration with dependency often uses imperfect subjunctive in the time clause
naturalExamples: Avisame cuando tengas la informacion.
Cenaremos cuando Juan llegue.
En cuanto reciba tu mensaje, te llamo.
Siempre ceno cuando termino de trabajar.
Limpio la cocina antes de que lleguen los invitados.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 50: Non-Specific Relative Clauses (Subjunctive)
lessons.targetPattern: Use subjunctive in relative clauses with que when the referent is non-specific, and use indicative when the referent is specific and identified.
lessons.englishTrap: Using the same relative-clause mood regardless of whether the referent is known or unknown.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish marks non-specific desired or searched referents with subjunctive, while known identified referents take indicative.
formula: non-specific referent: indefinite article plus que plus subjunctive
specific referent: definite article plus que plus indicative
common triggers include querer buscar necesitar preferir with non-specific targets
non-specific person templates: busco a alguien que sepa or pueda
naturalExamples: Quiero una silla que sea comoda.
Quiero la silla que es comoda.

Busco la casa que da al parque.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 49: Ojala for Hopes vs Wishes
lessons.targetPattern: Use ojala with present subjunctive for realistic hopes and with imperfect subjunctive for unlikely or hypothetical wishes.
lessons.englishTrap: Using one single I hope or I wish structure without changing mood by meaning.
lessons.spanishLogic: Ojala always triggers subjunctive, but the tense choice signals whether the outcome is plausible or hypothetical.
formula: ojala que plus present subjunctive for likely present or near-future outcomes
ojala que plus imperfect subjunctive for unlikely or unreal wishes
que is optional after ojala
common pair contrasts: haya versus hubiera, pueda versus pudiera, este versus estuviera
naturalExamples: Ojala no haya mucho trafico.
Ojala tengamos tiempo para descansar.
Ojala pueda ayudarte hoy.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 48: Para Que for Purpose and Intent
lessons.targetPattern: Use para plus infinitive with same subject and para que plus subjunctive with subject change, including imperfect subjunctive after past main clauses for intended outcomes.
lessons.englishTrap: Using one to plus verb structure for every purpose expression.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish purpose clauses split by subject continuity and tense alignment across clauses.
formula: same subject: para plus infinitive
subject change present or future meaning: para que plus present subjunctive
subject change after past main verb with would meaning: para que plus imperfect subjunctive
commands can take para que plus subjunctive purpose clauses
naturalExamples: Trabajo todos los dias para ahorrar dinero.
Hago videos para que mejores tu espanol.
Cierra la puerta para que el gato no se escape.
Pinte la casa para que se vendiera mas rapido.
Le explique todo para que supiera la verdad.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 47: Subjunctive Trigger Verbs with Subject Change
lessons.targetPattern: Use trigger verb plus que plus present subjunctive when subjects differ, and use infinitive when subjects stay the same.
lessons.englishTrap: Translating want, hope, prefer, recommend, and insist without checking subject change.
lessons.spanishLogic: Subjunctive appears after trigger verbs when the second verb has a different subject; same subject uses infinitive.
formula: querer que plus subjunctive
esperar que plus subjunctive
desear que plus subjunctive
preferir que plus subjunctive
recomendar que plus subjunctive
insistir en que plus subjunctive
same subject uses trigger plus infinitive
naturalExamples: Quiero que estudies espanol.
Espero que te mejores pronto.
Prefiero que te quedes en casa.
Recomiendo que te acuestes temprano.
Insisto en que me digas la verdad.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 46: Antes de que vs Antes de
lessons.targetPattern: Use antes de que plus present subjunctive when subjects change, and use antes de plus infinitive when the subject stays the same.
lessons.englishTrap: Using a single before plus verb pattern in all contexts.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish distinguishes between subject change and no subject change, requiring either antes de que plus subjunctive or antes de plus infinitive.
formula: subject change uses antes de que plus present subjunctive
same subject uses antes de plus infinitive
rule stays the same whether phrase appears first or in the middle
present subjunctive used even when action is future in meaning
naturalExamples: Debo terminar esto antes de que llegue mi jefe.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 45: Present Subjunctive Production Drills
lessons.targetPattern: Use trigger plus que plus present subjunctive across regular, stem-changing, spelling-changing, and irregular verbs.
lessons.englishTrap: Using present indicative after desire, doubt, recommendation, or emotion triggers.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish switches mood in subordinate clauses after trigger expressions and uses present subjunctive forms built from the yo stem.

ar endings e es e emos en
er ir endings a as a amos an
stem changes as in present with subjunctive distribution
spelling changes for car gar zar

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 44: Conditional for Would, Could, and Politeness
lessons.targetPattern: Use conditional tense for hypothetical results, politeness, and future-in-the-past reporting, including regular and irregular stems.
lessons.englishTrap: Using present or future forms for every would or could meaning.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish conditional marks polite distance, hypothetical outcomes, and reported future from a past perspective.
formula: infinitive plus conditional endings ia ias ia iamos ian
irregular stems tendr podr dir har querr pondr saldr vendr sabr
politeness: podria podrias me gustaria
future in past: dijo que llegaria
hypothetical frame: si plus imperfect subjunctive then conditional
naturalExamples: Hablariamos mas si tuvieramos tiempo.
Podrias ayudarme, por favor?
Me gustaria reservar una mesa.
Juan dijo que llegaria a las cinco.
Yo me mudaria a Mexico si fuera rico.

Tu comerias mas verduras.
El viviria en Mexico.
Nosotros estudiariamos mas si tuvieramos tiempo.
Ellos trabajarian aqui.
Usted aprenderia espanol rapidamente.

Me dijeron que llegarian tarde.
Seria posible cambiar la cita?

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 43: Poner and Ponerse in Real Use
lessons.targetPattern: Use poner across placement, commands, subjunctive triggers, indirect-object food and drink frames, emotional impact, and pronominal ponerse for becoming or putting on clothing.
lessons.englishTrap: Treating poner as only to put and translating each use literally.
lessons.spanishLogic: Poner is a flexible high-frequency frame across several constructions, and ponerse changes meaning to become or put on.
formula: preterite placement: puse pusiste puso pusimos pusieron
commands: pon no pongas ponga no ponga
subjunctive trigger: quieres que ponga
indirect object frame: que le pusiste a and le puse
emotional impact: me puso triste or me ponen nervioso
pronominal: ponerse plus adjective or clothing
naturalExamples: Donde pusiste las llaves?
Pon las maletas en el coche.
Quieres que ponga la mesa?
Que le pusiste a la sopa?
Me puse nervioso antes de la reunion.

I became happy when I saw you yesterday.

answers: Donde pusiste las llaves?
Puse las flores en la cocina.
Pusimos los platos en la mesa.
Ella puso el libro en su mochila.

Quieres que ponga las maletas en el coche?
Quieres que pongamos la ropa en la lavadora?
Que le pusiste a la sopa?
Le puse sal.
Que le pusieron a la ensalada?
Las noticias me pusieron triste.
Las mentiras me ponen furioso.
Me puse feliz al verte ayer.
Nos pusimos nerviosos durante el examen.

Nos ponemos los abrigos en invierno.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 42: Relative Pronouns for Natural Linking
lessons.targetPattern: Link clauses naturally with que, quien(es), el/la/los/las que, and lo que while keeping required prepositions before the relative pronoun.
lessons.englishTrap: Dropping relative pronouns and leaving prepositions at sentence ends.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish keeps explicit relative links and places prepositions before the relative pronoun.
formula: noun plus que plus clause as default
preposition plus quien or quienes for people
preposition plus el/la/los/las que with agreement
lo que for ideas and what clauses
no dangling prepositions in Spanish
naturalExamples: El libro que lei fue util.
La mujer con quien trabaje vive cerca.
La persona de la que te hable llego hoy.
Lo que necesito es tiempo.
Ella llego tarde, lo cual sorprendio a todos.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 41: Preterite vs Imperfect in Real Stories
lessons.targetPattern: Choose preterite for completed events and imperfect for background, habitual, and ongoing context, including verbs whose meaning shifts by tense.
lessons.englishTrap: Using one past form for every situation and translating was, knew, could directly.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish separates completed story events from background context, and several high-frequency verbs shift meaning depending on preterite vs imperfect.
formula: completed event uses preterite
background, habit, and ongoing state use imperfect
interruption pattern: imperfect plus cuando plus preterite
meaning shifts: conoci and conocia, supe and sabia, quise and queria, pude and podia, tuve and tenia
naturalExamples: Cuando era nino, iba a la playa cada verano.
Ayer lei un libro interesante.
Estabamos en el parque cuando empezo a llover.
Conoci a Juan en 2010, pero Maria ya lo conocia.
Yo podia trabajar el sabado, pero no pude ir.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 40: Preterite vs Imperfect Decision Frames
lessons.targetPattern: Choose imperfect for background, description, age, time, and repeated past actions; choose preterite for completed events and interruptions; combine both to narrate natural past stories.
lessons.englishTrap: Using one generic past tense for everything and translating by vocabulary instead of event function.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish splits past meaning into scene/background (imperfect) and completed events or interruptions (preterite). Pick tense by role in the story.
formula: background, description, habitual past -> imperfect
single completed event with clear boundary -> preterite
ongoing past interrupted by event -> imperfect + preterite
time, age, weather, states in scene-setting -> usually imperfect
optional used-to emphasis -> soler in imperfect + infinitive
naturalExamples: Cuando era nino, vivia cerca del centro.
Ayer llegue tarde a la reunion.
Estudiaba cuando me llamaste.
Eran las ocho y hacia frio.
Mi abuelo solia caminar todas las mananas.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 39: Preterite for Completed Actions
lessons.targetPattern: Use preterite for completed past actions with time anchors, including regular endings, high-frequency irregulars, and key spelling or stem shifts.
lessons.englishTrap: Using one generic past form without marking completed actions.
lessons.spanishLogic: Preterite packages an action as finished, especially with specific past-time cues such as ayer, anoche, and el ano pasado.
formula: completed action plus specific time marker uses preterite
regular ar endings: e, aste, o, amos, aron
regular er and ir endings: i, iste, io, imos, ieron
frequent irregulars: fui, hice, tuve, estuve, pude, dije, vine, vi, di
yo spelling shifts and third-person ir stem shifts
naturalExamples: Comi tacos anoche.
Fui a la tienda ayer.
Le dije la verdad.
Estuvimos en casa toda la noche.
Ellos durmieron poco.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 38: Commands That Sound Natural
lessons.targetPattern: Use imperative forms by addressee and polarity, and place object pronouns correctly: attached in affirmative commands and before the verb in negative commands.
lessons.englishTrap: Using modal or future-style statements instead of direct imperative forms.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish marks commands with dedicated verb forms and shifts pronoun position depending on affirmative or negative polarity.
formula: tu affirmative: present el/ella form or irregular haz, di, ve, pon, sal, se, ten, ven
tu negative: no plus present subjunctive
usted and ustedes commands: present subjunctive forms
nosotros command: subjunctive for both affirmative and negative
affirmative pronouns attach, negative pronouns go before the verb
RID order: reflexive then indirect then direct
naturalExamples: Pon la mesa, por favor.
No salgas sin chaqueta.
Senor, espere aqui un momento.
Apaguen sus telefonos, por favor.
Digamos la verdad hoy.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 37: Future Without Overthinking
lessons.targetPattern: Use three future frames naturally: ir a plus infinitive, present plus future time, and simple future, while recognizing irregular future stems and the supposition use of future tense.
lessons.englishTrap: Using only going to translations and avoiding Spanish future forms.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish uses three future paths naturally and uses simple future also for probability guesses.
formula: ir conjugated plus a plus infinitive
present tense plus future time expression
simple future: infinitive plus e, as, a, emos, an
irregular stems: tendr, dir, har, saldr, vendr, habr
supposition: sera, estara, tendra
naturalExamples: Voy a estudiar manana.
Trabajo el sabado.
Hablare con Maria.
Tendremos reunion a las nueve.
Estara ocupado ahora.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 36: Que Tal for Check-ins and Suggestions
lessons.targetPattern: Use que tal plus noun for how is or was check-ins and que tal si plus present tense for natural how about suggestions.
lessons.englishTrap: Building full questions with extra verbs for every how is or how was sentence.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish often uses compact frames: que tal plus noun for check-ins and que tal si plus present for suggestions.
formula: que tal plus noun
que tal plus possessive plus noun
que tal si plus present nosotros
short response chunks such as bien, muy bien, regular, fatal
naturalExamples: Que tal la comida?

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 35: Plug-and-Play Starters + Infinitive
lessons.targetPattern: Use high-frequency starter frames with one conjugated first verb plus infinitive, and adjust pronominal endings from se to me or te based on subject.
lessons.englishTrap: Conjugating both verbs when expressing want, have to, can, should, could, going to, or just did.
lessons.spanishLogic: Only the first verb is conjugated in these frames; the second verb remains infinitive. With pronominal verbs, replace se with me or te and attach it to the infinitive.
formula: quiero plus infinitive
tengo que plus infinitive
puedo plus infinitive
deberia or deberias plus infinitive
podria or podrias plus infinitive
voy a or vas a plus infinitive
acabo de or acabas de plus infinitive
pronominal shift: se to me or te
naturalExamples: Quiero terminar este informe.
Tengo que llamar al cliente.
Puedo salir mas tarde.
Voy a acostarme temprano.
Acabas de levantarte.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 34: Que vs Cual Without Guesswork
lessons.targetPattern: Select que for open what questions and definitions, use cual or cuales for choosing specific options and specific identity data with ser, and use cual(es) de for defined sets.
lessons.englishTrap: Directly mapping English what and which without structural context.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish interrogatives are pattern-driven by function, not direct lexical translation.
formula: que plus verb for general what
cual or cuales plus verb for option selection
cual or cuales de plus group noun for defined set
que plus ser for definitions and explanations
cual plus ser for specific identity data
naturalExamples: Que necesitas ahora?
Cual prefieres?
Cuales de estos libros quieres?
Que es la inflacion?
Cual es tu numero de telefono?

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 33: Gerundio Patterns for Real Conversation
lessons.targetPattern: Use gerundio forms only for verbal action patterns (not noun uses), including estar, seguir, llevar, terminar/acabar, andar, method expressions, perception verbs, and simultaneous action frames.
lessons.englishTrap: Using Spanish gerundio where English uses ing as a noun.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish infinitives express noun-like actions, while gerundio forms mark actions in progress or linked action framing.
formula: estar + gerundio
seguir + gerundio
llevar + duration + gerundio
terminar or acabar + gerundio
method/perception/simultaneity uses with gerundio
naturalExamples: Estoy leyendo un informe.
Sigo estudiando cada noche.
Llevo dos anos trabajando aqui.
Terminamos comiendo en casa.
Vi a Juan corriendo.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 32: Present Progressive Without Overusing It
lessons.targetPattern: Build present progressive with estar + gerundio, apply common irregular gerunds correctly, and choose between progressive and simple present based on natural usage context.
lessons.englishTrap: Overusing progressive and forcing it onto motion verbs and habitual actions.
lessons.spanishLogic: Progressive marks in-progress action, but Spanish often prefers simple present when context is clear.
formula: estar conjugated + gerundio
ar endings go to ando, er/ir endings go to iendo
irregular gerunds include diciendo, pidiendo, durmiendo, leyendo, oyendo, siguiendo
use estar tratando de + infinitive for trying to do something
prefer simple present with common motion verbs in basic conversation
naturalExamples: Estoy hablando con mi jefe.
Ella esta leyendo un informe.
Estamos oyendo un ruido raro.

Estoy tratando de aprender espanol.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 31: Present Tense for Now, Habit, Near Future, and Polite Requests
lessons.targetPattern: Use simple present as default for current actions, habits, near-future plans with time markers, and polite requests, while limiting unnecessary progressive constructions.
lessons.englishTrap: Overusing progressive and future forms where Spanish prefers simple present.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish present tense covers now, routine, near-future with markers, and polite request frames naturally.
formula: simple present for now and general current action
present plus frequency marker for habits
present plus future time marker for near future
me plus present verb for polite request
present with motion verbs, voy/viene, for immediate action

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 30: Por vs Para in Real Sentences
lessons.targetPattern: Select para for purpose, destination, recipient, and deadlines; select por for cause, motive, substitution/exchange, movement through, and duration.
lessons.englishTrap: Treating all English for meanings as one Spanish preposition.
lessons.spanishLogic: Por and para split by function: para points toward goal/recipient, por explains reason/path/duration.
formula: para + infinitive for purpose
para + person/place/time for recipient/destination/deadline
por + noun for cause/motive
por + place/time for through and duration
para que + subjunctive when subject changes
naturalExamples: Trabajo mucho para ahorrar dinero.
Gracias por tu ayuda.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 29: HABER for "Have," "There Is," and "Hay Que"
lessons.targetPattern: Use haber in three high-frequency systems: auxiliary perfect tenses, impersonal existence forms (hay/habia/hubo/habra), and impersonal obligation with hay que + infinitive.
lessons.englishTrap: Treating haber as possession have and translating directly.
lessons.spanishLogic: Haber is a structural verb for perfect tenses and impersonal existence/obligation, while tener handles possession.
formula: present perfect: haber + participio
past perfect: habia + participio
existence: hay, habia, hubo, habra
impersonal obligation: hay que + infinitive
hay is fixed for singular and plural

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 28: Color Agreement Without Translation Traps
lessons.targetPattern: Place color adjectives after nouns, apply agreement rules by color type, use shade patterns claro/oscuro after the color, and leverage color + noun construction when agreement is uncertain.
lessons.englishTrap: Keeping one fixed color form and English adjective order.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish color words follow noun position and rule-based agreement patterns, with safe alternatives for invariable forms.
formula: noun + variable color
number-only colors: azul/verde/gris
invariable colors: naranja/cafe/purpura/violeta
shade suffix after color: verde claro, azul oscuro
safe fallback: color + color word

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 27: Muy vs Mucho Without Guessing
lessons.targetPattern: Choose muy for adjective/adverb intensification, mucho as adverb with verbs and as standalone reply, and mucho/a/os/as as adjective agreeing with nouns.
lessons.englishTrap: Using one word for both very and a lot in every context.
lessons.spanishLogic: The form depends on what you modify: adjective/adverb, verb, noun, or standalone response.
formula: muy + adjective or adverb
verb + mucho
standalone answer: si, mucho
mucho + noun with agreement
do not use standalone muy in this core pattern
naturalExamples: El reporte esta muy claro.
Trabajo mucho los lunes.
Si, mucho.
Hay mucha comida en la oficina.
Tenemos muchos clientes nuevos.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 26: Pronominal Verbs Inside Core Patterns
lessons.targetPattern: Use pronominal infinitives naturally inside high-frequency frames (tener que, querer, ir a, poder), selecting the correct reflexive pronoun and preserving body-part article patterns and required prepositions like quejarse de.
lessons.englishTrap: Treating the se part as optional or leaving pronouns in English-like positions.
lessons.spanishLogic: The reflexive pronoun must match subject and stay attached to the infinitive in these frame drills.
formula: tener que + pronominal infinitive
querer + pronominal infinitive
ir a + pronominal infinitive
poder + pronominal infinitive
quejarse de + noun

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 25: Pronominal Verbs by Function
lessons.targetPattern: Choose the right pronominal function in context: true reflexive with body-part articles, reciprocal in plural subjects, inherently pronominal chunks (quejarse de, arrepentirse de), passive se for impersonal/public statements, and meaning-change pairs like ir/irse and dormir/dormirse.
lessons.englishTrap: Treating every se verb as the same and translating word-for-word from English pronoun logic.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish uses pronominal forms for different functions. You choose the pattern by meaning, not by memorizing one English equivalent for se.
formula: true reflexive: pronoun + verb + definite article body part (me lavo las manos)
reciprocal: plural subject + se + plural verb (nos ayudamos)
inherently pronominal chunk: quejarse de, arrepentirse de
passive se: se + third-person verb agreeing with noun (se venden boletos)
meaning shift pairs: dormir vs dormirse, ir vs irse
naturalExamples: Me lavo la cara antes de salir.
Nos ayudamos cuando hay mucho trabajo.
Me quejo del ruido del trafico.
Se venden boletos en linea.
Me dormi en el sofa y luego me fui tarde.

controlled.instructions: Write each in Spanish using the target pattern that best matches the meaning.
prompts: I wash my hands before lunch.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 24: TENER Chunks That Replace "To Be"
lessons.targetPattern: Use tener + noun chunks for personal states and age, use tener ganas de + infinitive for desire, use hacer for ambient weather temperature, and use estar + adjective for object temperature and spiciness.
lessons.englishTrap: Overusing estar for states that Spanish packages with tener.
lessons.spanishLogic: High-frequency personal states are expressed as possession chunks in Spanish, while weather and object temperature use different verbs.
formula: tener + noun chunk for personal state
tener + number + anos for age
tener ganas de + infinitive for desire
hacer calor/frio for weather
estar + adjective for object temperature and picante
naturalExamples: Tengo mucha hambre despues del trabajo.
Ella tiene 32 anos.
Tenemos ganas de salir esta noche.
Hace mucho calor hoy.
El cafe esta muy caliente.

Tengo sueno.
Tengo prisa.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 23: How Long, Since When, and How Long Ago
lessons.targetPattern: Express timelines with five high-frequency structures: hace + preterite for ago events, desde + start point, desde hace + duration, hace + duration + que + present, and llevar + duration + gerundio.
lessons.englishTrap: Using one English for/since/ago mapping for every timeline sentence.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish chooses different frames depending on whether you mean ago, since-point, or ongoing duration.
formula: hace + time expression + preterite
present tense + desde + specific point
present tense + desde hace + duration
hace + duration + que + present tense
llevar + duration + gerundio
naturalExamples: Llegue aqui hace tres anos.
Vivo en esta ciudad desde 2021.
Trabajo remoto desde hace dos anos.
Hace seis meses que estudio contabilidad.
Llevo veinte minutos esperando al cliente.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 22: Time Patterns That Sound Natural
lessons.targetPattern: Use fixed Spanish time chunks naturally: weekdays with article except after ser, months with en, dates with el + day + de + month, event times with a la/a las, and relative time blocks like proximo/pasado and por la manana/tarde/noche.
lessons.englishTrap: Mixing day, month, date, and clock patterns by translating from English.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish time is built with stable chunks that must be memorized and reused as full frames.
formula: Day reference: el lunes; after ser: Hoy es lunes
Month reference: en diciembre
Date: el 22 de septiembre
Event time: a la una / a las ocho y media
Relative time: la proxima semana / el mes pasado / por la manana
naturalExamples: Tengo reunion el martes por la manana.
Nos mudamos en enero.
Mi cita es el 14 de junio.
La clase empieza a las ocho y media.
A que hora sales hoy?

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 21: Definite Articles Where English Omits "The"
lessons.targetPattern: Use definite articles in Spanish where English often omits them: generic concepts, gustar categories, weekdays (except after ser), body parts/clothing with obvious ownership, titles when talking about someone, street names, and languages as concepts.
lessons.englishTrap: Dropping el/la/los/las because English often omits the article.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish article usage is pattern-based by context, not a direct English mirror.
formula: Generic concept: La salud es importante.
Gustar + category: Me gusta la musica. / Me gustan los perros.
Day (not after ser): Nos vemos el lunes. vs Hoy es lunes.
Body/clothing obvious owner: Me duele el brazo. / Ponte la chaqueta.
Talking about title/family member takes article; direct address drops it.
naturalExamples: La educacion mejora la calidad de vida.
Me gusta la musica en vivo.
Tenemos reunion el martes.
Me duele la espalda hoy.
El ingeniero Soto llega a las nueve.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 20: The Personal "A" in Real Sentences
lessons.targetPattern: Use the personal a before direct-object people and pets, omit it with inanimate direct objects, apply specificity rules with buscar/necesitar/contratar/encontrar, and avoid personal a with tener in normal possession meaning.
lessons.englishTrap: Skipping a before people because English has no equivalent marker.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish marks many direct-object people and pets with personal a, but not things, and not normally with tener.
formula: ver/conocer/llamar/visitar/ayudar/respetar + a + person or pet
buscar/necesitar/contratar + non-specific person (often no a)
buscar/necesitar + specific person/group -> use a
tener + person/pet (normally no personal a)
a alguien / a nadie usually takes personal a
naturalExamples: Veo a Marta en la entrada.
Buscamos un tecnico para el turno de noche.
Necesitamos a los tecnicos que conocen este sistema.
Tengo dos hijas y un perro.
No conozco a nadie en esa empresa.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 19: Ownership Without Word-for-Word Translation
lessons.targetPattern: Express ownership naturally with de quien + es/son, use possessive adjectives before nouns (mi, tu, su), and switch to article + possessive pronouns when replacing nouns (el mio, la tuya, los suyos), clarifying su/suyo with de + person when needed.
lessons.englishTrap: Thinking in direct English ownership order and translating whose literally.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish asks ownership with de quien + es/son and uses different forms for before nouns versus replacing nouns.
formula: De quien + es + singular noun
De quien + son + plural noun
No, no es mio/mia. Es de + name.
No, no son mios/mias. Son de + name.
Use de el/de ella/de ellos to clarify su/suyo when ambiguous.
naturalExamples: De quien es este cargador?
De quien son estos documentos?
No, no es mio. Es de Laura.
No, no son mias. Son de mi jefe.
Es suyo o de ella?

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 18 (Writing-First)

formula: IO + DO + preterite of dar
le/les + lo/la/los/las -> se + lo/la/los/las

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 17 (Writing-First)
lessons.targetPattern: Differentiate emotional verb patterns: gustar/encantar/fascinar for things and activities, caer bien/mal for interpersonal non-romantic liking, and querer/amar for loving people and pets.
lessons.englishTrap: Using one like or love verb for all emotional contexts.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish selects different structures based on meaning: preference for things, social chemistry with people, and direct love.
formula: me/te/le/nos/les + gusta(n)/encanta(n)/fascina(n) + thing/activity
a X + le cae bien/mal + person
querer/amar + a + person or pet
naturalExamples: Me encanta viajar.
A Ana le gustan las fresas.
No me cae bien mi jefe.
Quiero a mi familia.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 15 (Writing-First)
lessons.targetPattern: Use direct object pronouns (lo/la/los/las, plus me/te/nos) with correct agreement and placement before conjugated verbs or attached to infinitives.
lessons.englishTrap: Repeating full nouns and copying English object position.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish replaces direct objects with pronouns and places them before conjugated verbs, or before/attached to infinitives.
formula: Pronoun + conjugated verb (Lo veo)
Conjugated + infinitive: pronoun before conjugated or attached to infinitive
Neuter lo for ideas/facts (No lo sé)
naturalExamples: ¿Conoces a Juan? Sí, lo conozco.

¿Ves las llaves? No, no las veo.
¿Quieres conocer a María? Sí, la quiero conocer.
¿Qué quieres hacer? No lo sé.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 14 (Writing-First)
lessons.targetPattern: Differentiate saber (facts, information, skills), saber cómo (method/process), and conocer (familiarity with people/places/things), including personal a with people.
lessons.englishTrap: Translating every know as one verb.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish splits know by meaning: saber for facts/skills, conocer for familiarity, and saber cómo for process.

saber cómo + infinitivo (method/steps)
conocer + (a + persona) / conocer + lugar-cosa

lessons.title: Lesson 11: Demonstratives — este, ese, aquel, esto, eso
lessons.targetPattern: Choose the correct demonstrative by gender, number, distance, pronoun use, or neutral idea reference.
lessons.englishTrap: English uses this/that/these/those with less gender pressure. Spanish forces gender and number for nouns/pronouns: este libro, esta mesa, esos documentos, aquellas casas. Use esto/eso only for unknown things or whole ideas, not before a noun.
lessons.spanishLogic: este/esta/estos/estas = this/these near me; ese/esa/esos/esas = that/those near you or already mentioned; aquel/aquella/aquellos/aquellas = that/those over there or distant in time. esto/eso are neutral: ¿Qué es esto? No entiendo eso.
formula: Identify noun gender: libro = masculine, mesa = feminine, documentos = masculine plural, casas = feminine plural.
Identify distance: este = near me, ese = near you/mentioned, aquel = far away or distant time.
If the noun is omitted, use the matching pronoun: Quiero este. Prefiero esa. No quiero aquellos.
If the thing is unknown or the speaker means an idea/situation, use esto or eso.
Time phrases are fixed by noun gender/number: esta semana, ese día, en ese momento, en aquel entonces.
naturalExamples: este libro
esta mesa
esos documentos
aquellas casas
Quiero este.
Prefiero esa.
No quiero aquellos.
¿Qué es esto?
No entiendo eso.
esta semana
ese día
en ese momento
en aquel entonces

commonErrors: Using esto before a noun: say este libro, not esto libro.
For feminine singular nouns, use esta/esa/aquella: esta mesa, esa casa, aquella silla.
For masculine plural nouns, use estos/esos/aquellos: estos libros, esos documentos, aquellos años.
For feminine plural nouns, use estas/esas/aquellas: estas mesas, esas casas, aquellas sillas.
Use esto/eso for unknown things, whole ideas, statements, or situations: No entiendo eso.
Use aquel/aquella/aquellos/aquellas for physical distance or distant time: en aquel entonces.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 10 (Writing-First)
lessons.targetPattern: Use demonstrative adjectives and pronouns (este/ese/aquel + gender/number forms, plus esto/eso) to point to specific objects, unknown concepts, and time references naturally.
lessons.englishTrap: Using one fixed this/that form without gender and number agreement.
lessons.spanishLogic: Demonstratives must match noun gender and number; neuter forms refer to ideas or unknown things.
formula: este/esta/estos/estas + noun (this/these)
ese/esa/esos/esas + noun (that/those)
aquel/aquella/aquellos/aquellas + noun (that/those over there)
naturalExamples: Quiero esta mesa.
No me gusta ese carro.
¿Ves aquellos edificios?
¿Qué es esto?
Quiero hablar de eso.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 9 (Writing-First)
lessons.targetPattern: Distinguish existence (hay) from location of known objects (está/están), and place objects naturally using core Spanish prepositions of place.
lessons.englishTrap: Using one is/are structure for everything.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish separates existence (hay) from location of known things (está/están).
formula: Hay + noun (there is / there are)
El/La + noun + está ... (specific singular thing is located)
Los/Las + noun + están ... (specific plural things are located)
naturalExamples: Hay una mesa en la cocina.
La mesa está en la cocina.
Hay llaves en el pasillo.
Las llaves están en el pasillo.
¿Hay una silla cerca de la ventana?

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 8 (Writing-First)
lessons.targetPattern: Use ir a + infinitive to express near-future intention, and contrast it with querer, poder, and tener que + infinitive.
lessons.englishTrap: Translating future ideas with direct present only, or mixing going to with go to a place.
lessons.spanishLogic: Near-future intention is expressed with ir a + infinitive as a stable action frame.
formula: Voy/Vas/Va/Vamos + a + infinitivo
¿Qué vas a ...? / ¿Cuándo vas a ...? / ¿Dónde vas a ...?
Contrast with quiero + infinitivo, puedo + infinitivo, tengo que + infinitivo
naturalExamples: Voy a descansar después del trabajo.
¿Vas a estudiar esta noche?
Vamos a comprar comida ahora.
¿Qué vas a hacer mañana?

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 7 (Writing-First)
lessons.targetPattern: Use ir in preterite (fui/fuiste) to report completed movement in the past, with question frames and time anchors like ya and hace + tiempo.
lessons.englishTrap: Using present-style movement sentences for past events, or translating ago/already loosely.
lessons.spanishLogic: For completed movement in the past, use ir in preterite (fui/fuiste) and anchor it with hace or ya.
formula: Fui/Fuiste + a + lugar
¿Fuiste ...? / ¿Cuándo fuiste ...? / ¿Adónde fuiste?
... hace + tiempo and ya + fui/fuiste
naturalExamples: Fui al banco hace una hora.
¿Fuiste al supermercado ayer?
Ya fui a la farmacia.
¿Cuándo fuiste al gimnasio?
No fui a la oficina el lunes.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 6 (Writing-First)
lessons.targetPattern: Use ir a + place and ir a + place + a + infinitive to express movement and purpose, and combine it with querer, poder, and tener que.
lessons.englishTrap: Translating to one-to-one and getting stuck when a sentence has two to ideas (go to X / to do Y).
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish motion uses ir a + place, and purpose uses a second a + infinitive.
formula: Voy/Vas + a + lugar
Quiero/Puedo/Tengo que + ir a + lugar
... ir a + lugar + a + infinitivo (purpose)
naturalExamples: Voy al supermercado.
Quiero ir a la biblioteca.
Tengo que ir al banco hoy.
¿Puedes ir a la farmacia conmigo?
Voy a la tienda a comprar pan.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 5 (Writing-First)
lessons.targetPattern: Use tener que + infinitive to express obligation, and contrast it with querer (desire) and poder (ability/permission).
lessons.englishTrap: Translating have to word-by-word and treating que as a separate literal meaning.
lessons.spanishLogic: Tener que is one fixed obligation chunk: conjugated tener + que + infinitive.
formula: Tengo que + infinitivo (I have to...)
Tienes que + infinitivo (you have to...)
Contrast frame: Quiero... / Puedo... / Tengo que...
naturalExamples: Tengo que trabajar mañana.
¿Tienes que confirmar la reserva hoy?
Tengo que verificar la información primero.
Quiero ayudarte, pero tengo que salir.
No puedo ahora; tengo que terminar este reporte.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 4 (Writing-First)
lessons.targetPattern: Use querer vs poder with infinitives to express desire, ability, and permission, including request frames with ayudarme, conmigo, and contigo.
lessons.englishTrap: Treating want to and can as the same kind of sentence.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish separates intention (querer) from ability or permission (poder). Fast production comes from using conjugated modal verb plus infinitive.
formula: Quiero/Quieres + infinitivo (desire)
Puedo/Puedes + infinitivo (ability or permission)
¿Me puedes ayudar a + infinitivo? / ¿Puedes ayudarme con + sustantivo?
naturalExamples: Quiero confirmar la reserva hoy.
¿Puedes ayudarme con el proyecto?
¿Puedo hablar contigo un momento?
No puedo trabajar el sábado.
Quiero colaborar contigo en el video.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 3 (Writing-First)
lessons.targetPattern: Narrate past events by combining imperfect for background/habit and preterite for completed events, including meaning shifts in verbs like poder, querer, saber, and conocer.
lessons.englishTrap: Using one generic past tense for every situation.
lessons.spanishLogic: Spanish past narration usually combines two layers: imperfect for context or ongoing action, and preterite for completed events that move the story forward.
formula: Imperfecto (contexto) + cuando/mientras + Pretérito (evento puntual)
Imperfecto for repeated or habitual past (siempre, de niño, todos los días)
Pretérito for finished actions with clear endpoint (ayer, anoche, de repente)
naturalExamples: Estaba en casa cuando sonó el teléfono.
Cuando era niño, iba al parque todos los sábados.
Mientras trabajaba, recibí un mensaje urgente.
Había mucho tráfico cuando salí de casa.
Quería llamarte, pero no pude.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 2 (Writing-First)
lessons.targetPattern: Use -ción cognate nouns with correct feminine articles (la/una/las/unas) and build action sentences with quiero/no quiero + infinitive + noun phrase.
lessons.englishTrap: Translating word-by-word and treating each -tion word as unrelated vocabulary.
lessons.spanishLogic: Many English -tion words map to Spanish -ción; these nouns are usually feminine, and they become useful quickly inside quiero/no quiero + infinitive frames.
formula: Quiero + infinitivo + la/una + sustantivo en -ción
No quiero + infinitivo + la/una + sustantivo en -ción
Las/unas + sustantivos en -ciones (article agreement)
naturalExamples: Quiero revisar la información antes de la reunión.
No quiero repetir la conversación de ayer.
Quiero confirmar la reservación hoy.

Las verificaciones toman tiempo, pero son necesarias.

lessons.title: Spanish Daily Lesson 1 (Writing-First)
lessons.targetPattern: Use high-frequency -al cognates naturally in Spanish frames: 'Sujeto + ser + adjetivo', 'Es + adjetivo + infinitivo', and 'En general + oración'.
lessons.englishTrap: Translating word-for-word and avoiding obvious cognates.
lessons.spanishLogic: Many English words ending in -al map directly to Spanish and become fluent when placed in Spanish sentence frames.
formula: Sujeto + ser + adjetivo en -al
Es + adjetivo en -al + infinitivo
En general, + oración
naturalExamples: Este contrato es legal.
La reunión de hoy es informal.
Es fundamental revisar el correo cada mañana.
En general, el pago digital es normal ahora.
Es natural tener dudas al principio.

controlled.instructions: Write each in Spanish using the target pattern. No answer key until the end.
prompts: This process is simple and legal.
The final decision is individual.
It is normal to feel tired after work.
The meeting is formal today.
Digital payment is now common.
It is fundamental to confirm the address.
In general, this neighborhood is quiet.
The principal problem is financial.
It is natural to have questions.
The service is terrible, but legal.
This policy is universal in our company.
The report is brief and professional.
It is important, but not urgent.
The social event is tomorrow.
The total cost is high.
Rural internet is still unstable.
The criminal case is complex.
The judicial process is slow.
It is normal to change your mind.
In general, the atmosphere here is informal.
answers: Este proceso es simple y legal.
La decisión final es individual.
Es normal sentirse cansado después del trabajo.
La reunión es formal hoy.
El pago digital es común ahora.
Es fundamental confirmar la dirección.
En general, este barrio es tranquilo.
El problema principal es financiero.
Es natural tener preguntas.
El servicio es terrible, pero legal.
Esta política es universal en nuestra empresa.
El informe es breve y profesional.
Es importante, pero no urgente.
El evento social es mañana.
El costo total es alto.
El internet rural todavía es inestable.
El caso criminal es complejo.
El proceso judicial es lento.
Es normal cambiar de opinión.
En general, el ambiente aquí es informal.

mutation.instructions: Transform each base sentence as directed.
prompts: Base: El contrato es legal. -> Make it negative.
Base: La reunión es formal. -> Turn it into a question.
Base: El proceso es normal. -> Change to plural subject (los procesos).
Base: Es fundamental revisar el correo. -> Change to past (was fundamental).
Base: El pago digital es común. -> Change to future (will be).
Base: En general, el ambiente es informal. -> Make it emphatic with muy.
Base: La decisión final es individual. -> Change to first person plural possession (our final decision).
Base: Es natural tener dudas. -> Make it negative.
Base: El problema principal es financiero. -> Change to question.
Base: El proceso judicial es lento. -> Change adjective to plural by making subject plural.
Base: La respuesta es total. -> Make it sound natural with completa instead of total.
Base: El trato es brutal. -> Soften register for work context.
Base: Es formal hablar así en ese contexto. -> Change to informal.
Base: La solución digital es funcional. -> Change to past (was not functional).
Base: En general, la comunicación es social. -> Rewrite with más profesional.
answers: El contrato no es legal.
¿La reunión es formal?
Los procesos son normales.
Fue fundamental revisar el correo.
El pago digital será común.
En general, el ambiente es muy informal.
Nuestra decisión final es individual.
No es natural tener dudas.
¿El problema principal es financiero?
Los procesos judiciales son lentos.
La respuesta es completa.
El trato es duro.
Es informal hablar así en ese contexto.
La solución digital no fue funcional.
En general, la comunicación es más profesional.

contrast.instructions: Write the most natural Spanish choice.
prompts: Express: I have to send the final report today. (tengo que vs es fundamental)
Express a general truth: It is normal to make mistakes at the start. (es normal vs está normal)
Express personal obligation: I have to attend the formal meeting. (tengo que vs hay que)
Express impersonal recommendation: You have to verify the legal data. (hay que vs tengo que)
Express ability: I can use the digital system now. (puedo vs tengo que)
Express future plan: I am going to review the principal points tonight. (voy a vs quiero)
Express desire only: I want a more informal environment. (quiero vs puedo)
Express state vs essence: The office is formal (in general). (es vs está)
Express temporary state: Today the office is unusually formal. (es vs está)
Express naturalness: It feels natural to speak Spanish every day now. (es natural vs literal translation)
answers: Tengo que enviar el informe final hoy.
Es normal cometer errores al principio.
Tengo que asistir a la reunión formal.
Hay que verificar los datos legales.
Puedo usar el sistema digital ahora.
Voy a revisar los puntos principales esta noche.
Quiero un ambiente más informal.
La oficina es formal.
Hoy la oficina está inusualmente formal.
Ahora es natural hablar español todos los días.

writing.instructions: Write one Spanish sentence for each specific real-life meaning.
prompts: Your work schedule is not flexible and is very formal.
It is fundamental for you to sleep well before a big meeting.
Your final decision about a purchase is individual.
Digital payment is normal in your daily life.
The principal problem this week is time.
It is natural for you to feel pressure before presentations.
In general, your communication style is informal with friends.
A legal process in your city is slow.
A social plan tonight is not possible for you.
The total cost of something you need is too high.
Rural transportation near you is limited.
It is fundamental to check your bank app daily.
A formal email is necessary in your current situation.
In general, your mornings are quiet and practical.
It now feels natural to write in Spanish every day.

reverse.instructions: Write natural Spanish; avoid literal English mapping.
prompts: In general, this is normal.
It is natural to feel this way.
The main thing is to finish today.
It is fundamental to be clear.
This process is legal but slow.
A formal tone is better here.
Digital is already the normal option.
The final part is the most important.
That reaction is not unusual.
It feels normal now, not forced.
answers: En general, esto es normal.
Es natural sentirse así.
Lo principal es terminar hoy.
Es fundamental ser claro.
Este proceso es legal, pero lento.
Aquí es mejor un tono formal.
Lo digital ya es la opción normal.
La parte final es la más importante.
Esa reacción no es rara.
Ahora se siente normal, no forzado.
